heroOverview: Sneezing — medically termed sternutation — is a forceful, reflex expulsion of air through the nose and mouth driven by irritation of the nasal mucosa, capable of propelling respiratory droplets at speeds up to 160 km/h. Virtually every person sneezes as a normal protective reflex, but persistent sneezing occurring in frequent bouts is the hallmark presentation of allergic rhinitis, which affects approximately 10-30% of adults worldwide and 40% of children according to the World Health Organization. Beyond allergy, recurrent sneezing signals common-cold viral infection, non-allergic rhinitis, or exposure to environmental irritants. Isolated bouts need no treatment; persistent sneezing with nasal congestion, itching, or discharge warrants investigation for an underlying allergic or structural aetiology to prevent complications including sinusitis, sleep disturbance, and impaired quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: Allergic rhinitis — the leading cause of recurrent sneezing — affects 10-30% of adults and up to 40% of children globally (WHO)
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; allergic rhinitis peaks in childhood and adolescence but can develop at any age
keyStats.avgAge: First presentation of allergic rhinitis most common in children aged 3-18 years
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 400 million people worldwide have allergic rhinitis; viral upper respiratory infections — another major sneezing cause — affect virtually everyone annually

definition: Sneezing (ICD-10: R06.7) is a semi-autonomous convulsive expulsion of air from the lungs through the nose and mouth, initiated by stimulation of the trigeminal nerve afferents in the nasal mucosa and coordinated by a sneezing centre located in the lateral medulla. The reflex arc begins when irritants — allergens, viral particles, dust, smoke, cold air, or bright light (photic sneeze reflex) — stimulate sensory nerve endings in the nasal epithelium. Afferent signals travel via the ophthalmic and maxillary branches of the trigeminal nerve to the medullary centre, which coordinates deep inspiration followed by glottic closure, buildup of intrathoracic pressure, then explosive expiration through both nose and mouth. The photic sneeze reflex — sneezing triggered by sudden bright light — is an autosomal dominant trait present in 18-35% of the population. As a clinical presentation (R06.7), sneezing becomes medically significant when it is persistent, occurs in bouts of ten or more consecutive sneezes, or is accompanied by nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, itching, post-nasal drip, or eye symptoms. The most important underlying causes to distinguish are allergic rhinitis, non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis, infectious (viral) rhinitis, and drug-induced rhinitis. Sneezing also serves as the primary mechanism of respiratory pathogen transmission, making it epidemiologically important beyond its clinical significance.

typesClassification.description: Sneezing driven by IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation in response to airborne allergens; seasonal disease is caused by pollens and mould spores, perennial disease by house dust mites, pet dander, and cockroach allergens.

Persistent sneezing and rhinorrhoea in the absence of demonstrable allergy; triggers include temperature changes, strong odours, smoke, and humidity shifts, mediated through autonomic hyperreactivity of the nasal vasculature.

Viral infection of the nasal epithelium causes oedema, increased secretion, and irritation of mucosal sensory nerve endings, producing sneezing, clear rhinorrhoea, and congestion as acute symptoms typically lasting 7-10 days.

An inherited trait causing sneezing on exposure to sudden bright light, affecting 18-35% of people; the mechanism involves aberrant cross-activation between the optic and trigeminal pathways at the brainstem level.

Rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion from topical decongestant overuse), NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease, and occupational exposure to flour, wood dust, chemicals, or latex can all cause persistent sneezing.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, forceful expulsion of air through the nose and mouth, often in runs of multiple consecutive sneezes with seconds between each.
Clear watery nasal discharge (rhinorrhoea) that accompanies or immediately follows sneezing, often profuse in allergic rhinitis.
Nasal congestion and stuffiness from mucosal oedema, reducing airflow through one or both nostrils.
Itching of the nose, palate, eyes, and ears — the classic quartet of allergic rhinitis that distinguishes it from infection.
Red, watery, itchy eyes (allergic conjunctivitis) co-occurring with nasal symptoms in up to 80% of allergic rhinitis patients.
Post-nasal drip — mucus draining down the back of the throat causing throat-clearing, cough, or a sore throat.
Reduced sense of smell (hyposmia) during active nasal congestion due to blockage of olfactory airflow.
Fatigue and sleep disruption when congestion and sneezing interfere with restful breathing at night.
Facial pressure or headache from sinus congestion secondary to prolonged nasal obstruction.
Dark circles under the eyes (allergic shiners) from venous congestion below the orbits in chronic allergic rhinitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Sneezing bouts that reliably occur at the same time each year, suggesting seasonal allergen exposure.
Sneezing that worsens indoors or after contact with animals, pointing to perennial allergen triggers.
Nasal itching and clear discharge beginning within minutes of allergen exposure.
Sneezing accompanying a sore throat and low-grade fever, suggesting early viral rhinitis.
emergencySigns: Forceful sneezing causing sudden severe chest pain or shortness of breath, which may indicate a rib fracture or pneumothorax in fragile bone or lung disease.
Sneezing accompanied by severe facial pain and high fever suggesting acute bacterial sinusitis or orbital cellulitis.
Sneezing in a patient who has recently undergone eye, ENT, or intracranial surgery — explosive pressure changes can disrupt healing surgical sites.
Anaphylaxis-associated sneezing immediately following allergen ingestion or injection, accompanied by urticaria, throat tightening, or hypotension.

causes.description: In people with allergic rhinitis, inhalation of pollen, house dust mite faeces, pet dander, or mould spores cross-links IgE antibodies on nasal mast cells, triggering histamine release that irritates trigeminal sensory nerve endings and initiates the sneeze reflex within minutes of exposure.

Rhinoviruses, coronaviruses, and influenza viruses infect nasal epithelial cells and provoke inflammatory oedema and increased mucus production, mechanically and chemically irritating nasal mucosal sensory receptors. This is the cause of the sneezing in the common cold.

Temperature changes (entering cold air from warmth), humidity shifts, strong odours, cigarette smoke, and pollution activate parasympathetic and sensory neural pathways in the nasal vasculature autonomously, without IgE mediation, causing sneezing and rhinorrhoea particularly in middle-aged and older adults.

causes.cause: Nasal mucosal irritants — dust, chemicals, and pollutants
causes.description: Fine particulate matter, occupational dust (wood, flour, grain), chemical fumes, and air pollution directly stimulate trigeminal afferents in the nasal epithelium, causing reflex sneezing as a protective expulsion mechanism. Occupational rhinitis affects workers in bakeries, woodworking, and chemical processing.

Inflammatory polyps obstructing the nasal passages cause mucosal irritation and altered airflow dynamics that chronically stimulate sneezing reflexes; polyps are commonly associated with aspirin intolerance and asthma in Samter's triad.

Prolonged use of topical alpha-adrenergic decongestant sprays such as oxymetazoline causes rebound mucosal congestion and hypersecretion when the drug wears off, perpetuating a cycle of sneezing and nasal blockage that continues until the spray is discontinued.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: A personal history of eczema or asthma, or a first-degree relative with allergic disease, increases the risk of allergic rhinitis — the most common cause of recurrent sneezing — by approximately two- to threefold, reflecting the polygenic inheritance of IgE-mediated sensitisation.

riskFactors.factor: Indoor allergen exposure (house dust mite, pet dander)
riskFactors.description: Living with cats, dogs, or carpeted bedrooms where house dust mites accumulate provides continuous exposure to perennial allergens, sustaining ongoing nasal sensitisation and daily sneezing episodes in atopic individuals.

Proximity to heavily pollinating trees, grasses, and weeds during pollen seasons drives the seasonal surges in allergic sneezing seen in temperate climates; climate change is extending pollen seasons in many regions.

Workers in bakeries, animal laboratories, healthcare, farming, and woodworking are exposed to high concentrations of occupational allergens and irritants, making occupational allergic or irritant rhinitis a significant cause of work-related sneezing and rhinitis.
Cigarette smoke is a direct mucosal irritant and adjuvant for allergen sensitisation; both active smoking and passive exposure increase rhinitis symptom severity and frequency of sneezing episodes.

Using oxymetazoline or xylometazoline sprays for more than three to five days leads to rhinitis medicamentosa with rebound congestion and sneezing each time the spray wears off, a self-perpetuating cycle.

The presence of another atopic condition — particularly asthma — indicates shared immune dysregulation and strongly predicts concomitant allergic rhinitis. Approximately 80% of asthmatics also have allergic rhinitis.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 5-17, who have the highest prevalence of allergic rhinitis and associated sneezing
Young adults aged 18-40 with established allergic disease
Workers in high-allergen or high-irritant occupations — bakers, farmers, laboratory animal workers
People living in areas with high pollen counts, particularly urban areas where warming extends seasons
Adults over 60, in whom non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis becomes relatively more prevalent than allergic rhinitis
Patients with asthma or eczema, who have co-existing allergic rhinitis in the majority of cases
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation begins with a careful history: the pattern of sneezing (seasonal vs. year-round), trigger identification, associated symptoms (itching, watery discharge, eye symptoms), medication history, occupational exposures, and home environment. A positive family or personal history of atopy strongly supports allergic rhinitis as the cause. Physical examination assesses for pale, boggy nasal mucosa and watery discharge (allergic), versus erythematous mucosa (infectious), versus clear mucosa with turbinate oedema (vasomotor). Nasal polyps, a deviated septum, and turbinate hypertrophy are assessed on anterior rhinoscopy. Skin-prick testing or allergen-specific serum IgE measurement (ImmunoCAP) confirms allergic sensitisation and identifies the responsible allergens, which guides avoidance and immunotherapy decisions. Total serum IgE and eosinophil count support but do not replace specific testing. Nasal cytology (smear for eosinophils) can distinguish allergic from non-allergic eosinophilic rhinitis. When occupational exposure is suspected, patch testing or occupational challenge may be needed. CT sinuses is reserved for patients with recurrent sinusitis, suspected polyps, or failure to respond to appropriate treatment. The key diagnostic distinction is allergic rhinitis (IgE-mediated, allergen-specific) versus non-allergic rhinitis (no IgE sensitisation, autonomic or structural cause) versus infection (acute, self-limited, infectious context), because management differs substantially.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies specific allergens driving IgE-mediated sensitisation by introducing small amounts of allergen extracts into the superficial skin and measuring the resulting wheal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A series of small drops of allergen extracts are placed on the forearm and a lancet gently pricks through each; results are read at 15 minutes as the size of the wheal and flare reaction. Antihistamines must be stopped 3-5 days before testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Blood-based allergen-specific IgE measurement that can be performed when skin testing is contraindicated (severe eczema, inability to stop antihistamines, dermographism).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results return in 1-3 days, reporting IgE levels in kUA/L for each allergen tested.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasal anterior rhinoscopy or nasal endoscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the nasal mucosa to identify polyps, turbinate hypertrophy, deviated septum, purulent discharge, or foreign bodies that are causing or contributing to sneezing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief clinic procedure; a light or thin flexible endoscope is passed into each nostril while you breathe through your mouth. Minimal discomfort; takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Total serum IgE and blood eosinophil count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports the diagnosis of atopic disease; elevated eosinophils and IgE levels increase the probability of allergic rhinitis but are not specific enough for diagnosis alone.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood test; results available within hours to a day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates the sinuses for chronic sinusitis, polyp extent, and anatomical variants such as septal deviation or concha bullosa when surgery is being considered or when medical treatment has failed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short scan (2-5 minutes); no contrast injection needed for sinus evaluation. Minimal radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms occupational or specific allergen causation by controlled nasal instillation of the suspected agent and objective measurement of nasal airflow response using rhinomanometry.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a specialist setting; baseline nasal flow is measured, then a dilute allergen or irritant is applied nasally, and symptoms and airflow are reassessed at intervals. Takes 30-60 minutes with safety monitoring.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of sneezing depends on the underlying cause. For allergic rhinitis — the most common cause of persistent sneezing — current ARIA (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma) guidelines recommend intranasal corticosteroids (INS) as first-line therapy for moderate-to-severe disease; they are the most effective class for reducing all nasal symptoms including sneezing, with a response seen in two to four weeks of consistent use. For mild or predominantly sneezing and watery rhinorrhoea, an oral or intranasal antihistamine provides rapid (within one to two hours) relief, though intranasal antihistamines are now preferred for their faster onset and local efficacy. The combination of intranasal corticosteroid and intranasal antihistamine (Dymista) outperforms either agent alone in moderate-to-severe disease and is recommended in ARIA step-up therapy. Decongestants (pseudoephedrine, oxymetazoline) relieve congestion but do not address sneezing well; topical decongestants must be limited to five days to avoid rhinitis medicamentosa. Allergen immunotherapy — subcutaneous (SCIT) or sublingual (SLIT) — is the only disease-modifying treatment, capable of inducing long-term allergen tolerance and reducing symptom load by approximately 30-40% versus placebo in randomised trials; it is indicated in moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis not controlled by pharmacotherapy. For non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis, intranasal ipratropium bromide (an anticholinergic) is effective for watery rhinorrhoea and sneezing triggered by cold air and odours. For viral rhinitis, treatment is supportive — saline irrigation, topical decongestants for short-term relief, and rest. Allergen avoidance measures, including mattress encasements, air purifiers with HEPA filters, and pet removal or restriction from bedrooms, reduce exposure and symptom burden in allergic disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroids (fluticasone, mometasone, budesonide)
medicalTreatments.description: The most effective pharmacological treatment for allergic rhinitis and the first-line recommendation in ARIA guidelines for persistent symptoms. They reduce nasal mucosal inflammation, oedema, and sensory nerve hypersensitivity, addressing sneezing, blockage, and rhinorrhoea. Used once or twice daily; full effect takes one to two weeks of consistent use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials demonstrate 35-50% reduction in total nasal symptom scores versus placebo; superior to oral antihistamines for congestion and comparable for sneezing and rhinorrhoea.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine)
medicalTreatments.description: Block H1 histamine receptors, providing rapid relief of sneezing, itching, and rhinorrhoea in allergic rhinitis within one to two hours. Second-generation agents are non-sedating or minimally sedating compared with first-generation antihistamines. Best for mild disease or as-needed use during allergen exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce sneezing symptoms by approximately 30-40% versus placebo in allergen challenge studies; less effective than intranasal steroids for nasal blockage.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal antihistamine (azelastine)
medicalTreatments.description: A topical H1 antagonist applied directly to the nasal mucosa; onset of action within 15-30 minutes, faster than oral antihistamines. Effective for both allergic and non-allergic rhinitis and combined with intranasal fluticasone propionate in the fixed-dose Dymista formulation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to oral antihistamines for sneezing and rhinorrhoea; the combination product Dymista reduces total nasal symptom score by approximately 20% more than either component alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous or sublingual)
medicalTreatments.description: The only disease-modifying treatment for allergic rhinitis; involves administering gradually increasing doses of the causative allergen to induce immune tolerance. SCIT is given as monthly injections over 3-5 years; SLIT is administered as daily sublingual tablets or drops. Indicated in moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis not controlled by pharmacotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show 30-40% reduction in symptom scores and 30-40% reduction in medication use versus placebo, with effects persisting for at least 3 years after treatment completion.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal ipratropium bromide
medicalTreatments.description: An anticholinergic agent that reduces glandular hypersecretion and sneezing triggered by autonomic stimulation; particularly effective for non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis where cold air, odours, and irritants are main triggers, and in older patients where vasomotor rhinitis predominates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces watery rhinorrhoea and sneezing in non-allergic rhinitis by approximately 30-40% versus placebo in controlled trials; minimal systemic absorption.

surgicalOptions.name: Turbinate reduction (radiofrequency or submucosal resection)
surgicalOptions.description: Reduces the volume of inferior turbinates causing persistent nasal obstruction and contributing to sneezing in chronic rhinitis when medical management has failed. Performed as a day procedure under local or general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reports improvement in nasal airflow and symptom scores in approximately 70-80% of carefully selected patients at one year, though evidence for long-term benefit is moderate-quality.

surgicalOptions.name: Nasal polypectomy and functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS)
surgicalOptions.description: Removes obstructing nasal polyps and opens sinus drainage pathways when polyps are a cause of chronic sneezing, blockage, and recurrent sinusitis that is refractory to intranasal corticosteroids. Often combined with biological therapy (dupilumab) for severe polyp recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Randomised data show 60-80% improvement in nasal symptom scores after surgery; polyp recurrence at 2-5 years without maintenance intranasal steroid is common.

alternativeTreatments.name: Saline nasal irrigation (neti pot or spray)
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily irrigation with isotonic or hypertonic saline clears allergens, irritants, and mucus from the nasal passages, mechanically reducing the mucosal load that triggers sneezing. Supported by multiple randomised trials as a safe, low-cost adjunct to pharmacotherapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: HEPA air purifiers and allergen-proof bedding
alternativeTreatments.description: Removing allergen reservoirs from the sleeping environment — mattress and pillow encasements for dust mite allergy, air filtration for pet and pollen exposure — reduces antigenic load and can measurably lower daily symptom scores in perennial rhinitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture for allergic rhinitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Several randomised controlled trials have found acupuncture modestly superior to sham acupuncture for allergic rhinitis symptoms including sneezing; effect size is modest but the intervention is low-risk and may suit patients seeking non-pharmacological options.

whySeeSpecialist: Most sneezing from the common cold resolves without specialist input. A referral to an allergist or immunologist is appropriate when allergic rhinitis is suspected, to confirm sensitisation and discuss allergen immunotherapy for disease modification. An ENT specialist should assess patients with nasal polyps, structural abnormality, or recurrent sinusitis. Persistent sneezing in a healthcare worker or occupational setting warrants an occupational medicine assessment to identify workplace allergens and guide safety measures.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified allergist or clinical immunologist for suspected allergic rhinitis, particularly if you want allergen identification and immunotherapy. ENT (otolaryngology) is the appropriate specialty for structural causes such as polyps, deviated septum, or turbinate hypertrophy. An allergist with access to nasal endoscopy and on-site skin-prick testing provides the most efficient evaluation. Ask whether the practice offers sublingual immunotherapy as an alternative to injections, and whether they coordinate care with ENT for overlapping structural and allergic disease.
preventionStrategies: Identify and minimise exposure to personal allergen triggers through skin-prick or serum IgE testing.
Use allergen-proof mattress and pillow encasements, and wash bed linen at 60°C weekly to reduce house dust mite load.
Keep pets out of bedrooms and use HEPA vacuum filters in homes with pets.
Monitor local pollen counts and keep windows closed during peak pollen times; wear wraparound sunglasses outdoors.
Avoid tobacco smoke, strong perfumes, and chemical irritants that trigger non-allergic sneezing.
Limit topical decongestant nasal sprays to five days to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa.
Consider allergen immunotherapy as a long-term preventive strategy in moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis.
lifestyleModifications: Shower and change clothes after outdoor activities during pollen season to remove allergen deposits.
Use air conditioning and HEPA air purifiers in the home to reduce indoor allergen concentrations.
Dry clothes indoors rather than on a line during pollen season to avoid depositing pollen on laundry.
Keep humidity between 40-50% to reduce dust mite proliferation; below 40% inhibits mite breeding.
Wash hands frequently during cold and flu season to reduce viral rhinitis episodes.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) may modestly reduce atopic inflammation
Adequate vitamin D, which supports immune regulatory function and may reduce allergic sensitisation
Local honey has anecdotal support for desensitisation to local pollens, though randomised evidence is limited
Probiotic-rich foods such as yoghurt and fermented products, which may beneficially modulate immune regulation
avoid: Alcoholic beverages, which dilate nasal blood vessels and worsen nasal congestion and rhinitis symptoms
Spicy foods during active rhinitis, as capsaicin can temporarily worsen rhinorrhoea in sensitised individuals
Foods that cross-react with pollen allergens (oral allergy syndrome) — raw apples, celery, and carrots in birch-pollen-sensitised individuals, for example

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is not contraindicated in allergic rhinitis and is recommended; physical activity causes sympathetic vasoconstriction that can temporarily improve nasal patency. Exercise outdoors should be timed to avoid peak pollen hours (typically 5-10 am for grass pollen) during the relevant season. Using a nasal antihistamine or intranasal corticosteroid before outdoor exercise sessions helps manage exercise-triggered symptoms. Swimming in indoor pools is generally well tolerated, though chlorine can irritate the nasal mucosa in some individuals.
dailyManagement: Use intranasal corticosteroid spray consistently every day, not just when symptoms are severe — steady-state effect takes one to two weeks of regular use.
Perform nasal saline irrigation each morning to clear overnight allergen accumulation and reduce morning sneezing bouts.
Check daily pollen forecasts and adjust outdoor activity and premedication timing accordingly.
Track symptom patterns in a diary to identify specific triggers and confirm response to treatment changes.
Replace air conditioning filters every three months and use HEPA filters where possible.
prognosis: For benign viral rhinitis, sneezing resolves within seven to ten days without treatment in virtually all adults. Allergic rhinitis is a chronic condition in most patients but is highly controllable: approximately 75-80% of patients with moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis achieve good symptom control with intranasal corticosteroids and antihistamines combined. Allergen immunotherapy modifies the underlying disease and induces lasting tolerance in 60-80% of patients who complete a full course; long-term follow-up studies show sustained benefit for at least three years after stopping treatment. A minority of patients — roughly 20-30% — develop more severe rhinitis or new allergen sensitisations over time, and up to one-third eventually develop asthma if the rhinitis is not adequately treated. Non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis is managed rather than cured; it tends to persist with fluctuating severity but rarely causes serious complications. Sneezing related to nasal polyps requires long-term management with intranasal steroids and possibly repeated surgery, as recurrence rates are high.
recoveryTimeline: Viral rhinitis resolves fully within seven to ten days; sneezing typically peaks at days two to four. Allergic rhinitis responds to intranasal steroids in one to two weeks but requires ongoing treatment throughout the allergen season or year-round for perennial disease. Full benefit from allergen immunotherapy takes six to twelve months to become apparent and continues to build over three to five years. Surgery for polyps or turbinate hypertrophy results in immediate improvement in nasal airflow, with post-operative swelling resolving over two to four weeks.
complications: Chronic sinusitis — prolonged nasal obstruction and impaired mucociliary clearance predispose to bacterial infection and sinusitis, affecting up to 25% of patients with poorly controlled allergic rhinitis.
Asthma development — untreated allergic rhinitis approximately doubles the risk of developing asthma; effective rhinitis treatment measurably reduces asthma exacerbation frequency.
Sleep disturbance and obstructive sleep apnoea — nasal congestion forces mouth breathing, reducing sleep quality and contributing to snoring and apnoeic episodes in susceptible individuals.
Eustachian tube dysfunction and otitis media — allergic inflammation can extend to the middle ear via the Eustachian tube, causing hearing impairment, particularly in children.
Reduced quality of life and productivity — severe allergic rhinitis impairs concentration and academic performance; studies show effect sizes on cognitive function comparable to a mild sedative.

supportResources.name: Asthma and Allergy Foundation of America — Rhinitis Overview
supportResources.description: Patient-facing educational resource on allergic and non-allergic rhinitis, triggers, and treatment options from a major U.S. patient advocacy organisation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
supportResources.description: UK guidance on hay fever and perennial allergic rhinitis: symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment including immunotherapy.

supportResources.name: WHO — Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma (ARIA)
supportResources.description: WHO-endorsed international guidelines for the management of allergic rhinitis developed by the ARIA working group.

sources.title: ARIA guidelines update 2020 — Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma. Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology, 2020
Wheatley LM, Togias A. Clinical practice: allergic rhinitis. New England Journal of Medicine, 2015
Scadding GK et al. BSACI guideline for the diagnosis and management of allergic and non-allergic rhinitis. Clinical and Experimental Allergy, 2017
Durham SR, Penagos M. Sublingual or subcutaneous immunotherapy for allergic rhinitis? Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology, 2016
NICE NG33 — Allergic rhinitis: diagnosis and management. NICE guideline, 2021 update
WHO — Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma (ARIA) global guidelines document
Colas C et al. Nasal saline irrigation for allergic rhinitis: a Cochrane meta-analysis of randomised trials

confusedWithConditions.name: Upper respiratory infection (common cold)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral rhinitis causes sneezing for 7-10 days with sore throat, fever, and coloured discharge; allergic rhinitis causes seasonal or trigger-related sneezing with clear watery discharge and itching but no fever.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasal polyps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polyps cause persistent bilateral nasal blockage and sneezing but typically without the itch or eye symptoms of allergy; they are diagnosed by rhinoscopy or imaging and often coexist with eosinophilic disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma involves lower airway bronchospasm causing wheeze and breathlessness; it commonly co-exists with allergic rhinitis but the sneezing and nasal symptoms are distinct upper-airway phenomena.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic rhinitis and hay fever
relatedConditions.relevance: The primary cause of recurrent sneezing in the general population; allergen-specific IgE sensitisation drives the reflex arc.

relatedConditions.name: Upper respiratory infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral rhinitis is the most common acute cause of sneezing; differentiating it from allergic rhinitis guides treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: Polyps mechanically and chemically irritate the nasal mucosa, causing chronic sneezing and blockage that often requires surgical treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent nasal mucosal inflammation from any cause produces ongoing sneezing, congestion, and rhinorrhoea; management depends on distinguishing allergic from non-allergic disease.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pansinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: A common complication of chronic allergic rhinitis; sinus inflammation perpetuates mucosal irritation and sneezing.

faqs.question: Why do I sneeze so much?
faqs.answer: Frequent sneezing in bouts is most commonly caused by allergic rhinitis — an immune response to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or pet dander. Other causes include viral colds, non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis, nasal polyps, or irritant exposure. If sneezing persists or recurs seasonally, an allergy evaluation is helpful.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for sneezing from allergies?
faqs.answer: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone, mometasone) are the most effective first-line treatment for allergy-related sneezing, reducing total nasal symptoms by 35-50% in trials. They must be used daily for one to two weeks before full benefit is apparent. Antihistamines provide faster but shorter relief.

faqs.question: Is sneezing a sign of COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Sneezing can be a symptom of COVID-19, particularly with the Omicron variant, where it appeared more commonly than in earlier variants. It is not specific to COVID-19 and can result from any viral rhinitis or allergy. If accompanied by fever, loss of taste or smell, or fatigue, testing for COVID-19 is appropriate.

faqs.question: Why do I sneeze when I look at bright lights?
faqs.answer: The photic sneeze reflex is an inherited trait present in 18-35% of the population where sudden bright light triggers sneezing. It arises from aberrant cross-activation between the optic nerve and trigeminal pathways in the brainstem. It is harmless but can be relevant for pilots and drivers emerging into bright sun.

faqs.question: Can sneezing damage anything in my body?
faqs.answer: Vigorous sneezing in healthy people is harmless. Rarely, forceful sneezing has caused rib fractures in people with osteoporosis, pneumothorax in those with emphysema, retinal detachment, or post-surgical wound disruption. Suppressing a sneeze by holding the nose closed can transiently raise middle-ear pressure.

faqs.question: What is allergic rhinitis and how does it cause sneezing?
faqs.answer: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated immune response in which exposure to specific allergens triggers mast cells in the nasal lining to release histamine and other mediators. These chemicals stimulate trigeminal sensory nerve endings, initiating the sneeze reflex. The result is bouts of sneezing with watery discharge, itching, and congestion.

faqs.question: Should I take antihistamines every day for sneezing?
faqs.answer: For persistent allergic rhinitis, daily antihistamines provide ongoing relief of sneezing and itching, but intranasal corticosteroids are preferred as daily maintenance therapy because they are more effective and act on all nasal symptoms including congestion. Antihistamines can be used daily during the allergen season or taken before anticipated exposure.

faqs.question: Can food cause sneezing?
faqs.answer: Spicy foods — particularly those containing capsaicin — can trigger sneezing in susceptible individuals through stimulation of TRPV1 receptors in the nasal mucosa. This is distinct from food allergy; if sneezing occurs immediately after eating any food along with hives, lip swelling, or breathing difficulty, an allergic evaluation is warranted.

faqs.question: Can I use a nasal spray every day for sneezing?
faqs.answer: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays are safe for daily long-term use and are the recommended maintenance treatment for persistent allergic rhinitis. Unlike decongestant nasal sprays, which should not be used for more than five days, corticosteroid sprays do not cause rebound congestion and can be used throughout the allergen season or year-round.

faqs.question: Do allergies get worse with age?
faqs.answer: Allergies can both improve and worsen with age. Children sometimes outgrow food allergies, and aeroallergen sensitivity can diminish after adolescence. However, new sensitisations can develop in adulthood, and non-allergic vasomotor rhinitis becomes more common with ageing. Climate change is also extending pollen seasons, worsening exposure in many regions.

faqs.question: What is allergen immunotherapy and does it help sneezing?
faqs.answer: Allergen immunotherapy involves giving gradually increasing doses of the allergen you are sensitive to in order to train the immune system toward tolerance. Both subcutaneous injections (SCIT) and sublingual tablets or drops (SLIT) reduce sneezing and total nasal symptoms by 30-40% and provide benefits lasting years after the treatment course is completed.

faqs.question: How is sneezing different from a cold and allergies?
faqs.answer: Viral cold sneezing lasts 7-10 days, is accompanied by sore throat and sometimes fever, and discharge becomes coloured. Allergic rhinitis sneezing recurs seasonally or on allergen exposure, produces clear watery discharge, causes itching of the nose and eyes, and has no fever. A doctor can confirm the cause with allergy testing.

faqs.question: Can sneezing spread infections?
faqs.answer: Yes. A single sneeze can project respiratory droplets up to 2 metres and fine aerosol particles further, containing viruses such as rhinovirus, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2. Covering the nose and mouth with an elbow rather than a hand, and washing hands frequently, reduces transmission to others.

faqs.question: Why do I sneeze more in the morning?
faqs.answer: Morning sneezing bouts are common in allergic rhinitis because overnight accumulation of allergens on bedding — particularly house dust mites — triggers a reaction on waking. Nasal secretions also pool in the lying position and drain on standing, stimulating sneezing. Using allergen-proof bedding and rinsing the nose with saline on waking helps.

faqs.question: Can stress make sneezing worse?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and alters immune regulation in ways that can worsen allergic inflammation, increasing histamine sensitivity in the nasal mucosa. Studies have shown that stress worsens allergic rhinitis symptom scores, including sneezing frequency, independent of allergen exposure.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for sneezing?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if sneezing lasts more than 10 days, is present most days of the week, significantly disrupts sleep or daily function, is accompanied by facial pain, coloured nasal discharge, or hearing loss, or if your symptoms are not controlled by over-the-counter antihistamines. Allergy testing and prescription therapies can improve control substantially.

faqs.question: What is vasomotor rhinitis?
faqs.answer: Vasomotor rhinitis is persistent sneezing and rhinorrhoea triggered by temperature changes, strong odours, smoke, humidity, or exercise — not by allergens. It is caused by autonomic hyperreactivity of the nasal blood vessels rather than IgE-mediated allergy. Allergy testing is normal. Intranasal ipratropium and saline irrigation are first-line treatments.

faqs.question: Can nasal polyps cause sneezing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nasal polyps are soft inflammatory growths in the nasal passages that cause chronic nasal obstruction, post-nasal drip, and sneezing. They are associated with aspirin sensitivity and asthma. Treatment requires intranasal corticosteroids or biological agents (dupilumab) and sometimes endoscopic surgery to remove the polyps and restore nasal airflow.

faqs.question: Is sneezing contagious?
faqs.answer: Sneezing itself is not contagious, but the respiratory droplets expelled can contain viruses. Seeing or hearing someone sneeze does not directly cause another person to sneeze. However, common cold and influenza viruses spread primarily through sneeze droplets, so close contact with a sneezing person raises infection risk.

faqs.question: Why do antihistamines make me drowsy?
faqs.answer: First-generation antihistamines (chlorphenamine, diphenhydramine) cross the blood-brain barrier and block central histamine receptors, causing sedation. Second-generation antihistamines such as cetirizine, loratadine, and fexofenadine are designed to minimally penetrate the brain, making them far less sedating and preferred for daily use.

metaDescription: Persistent sneezing is the hallmark of allergic rhinitis. Learn causes, from pollen to polyps, and effective treatments including nasal sprays. Reviewed.
